coredata.dc:description: Understanding photocatalytic processes of nanoclusters at the nanoscale remains a challenge. Here, we synthesized two kinds of gold nanoclusters stabilized by thiol ligands (AuNCs) and carbon dots (AuCDs) to study their interfacial kinetics in photocatalytic H2O2 production. Three operando testing methods, including transient photovoltage (TPV), transient potential scanning (TPS) and photo-induced current (TPC), are used to investigate the influence of carbon dots (CDs) on catalytic processes. While AuNCs and AuCDs show similar O2 adsorption abilities, AuCDs exhibit a lower reaction rate due to less surface effective photoelectrons. The AuNCs utilize their own ligands to consume photo-generated holes while the presence of CDs in AuCDs changes this way and providing stable catalytic sites for water oxidation reaction. Additionally, with the addition of CDs, the oxygen reduction reaction pathway changes from a two-step one electron process to a one-step two electron process. This work provides new insights for understanding the photocatalytic processes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding photocatalytic processes of nanoclusters at the nanoscale remains a challenge. Here, we synthesized two kinds of gold nanoclusters stabilized by thiol ligands (AuNCs) and carbon dots (AuCDs) to study their interfacial kinetics in photocatalytic H2O2 production. Three operando testing methods, including transient photovoltage (TPV), transient potential scanning (TPS) and photo-induced current (TPC), are used to investigate the influence of carbon dots (CDs) on catalytic processes. While AuNCs and AuCDs show similar O2 adsorption abilities, AuCDs exhibit a lower reaction rate due to less surface effective photoelectrons. The AuNCs utilize their own ligands to consume photo-generated holes while the presence of CDs in AuCDs changes this way and providing stable catalytic sites for water oxidation reaction. Additionally, with the addition of CDs, the oxygen reduction reaction pathway changes from a two-step one electron process to a one-step two electron process. This work provides new insights for understanding the photocatalytic processes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Understanding the catalytic processes at the nanoscale is crucial for photoelectrochemical process based energy conversion. Two kinds of gold nanoclusters were synthesized and their photocatalytic activities were also studied in this work. Notably, the operando TPC, TPS, TPV tests jointly show the pathway of half reaction, dynamics of the interfacial charge distribution and kinetics of the interfacial charge transfer of these two catalysts at the nanoscale.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis, as one of the most ubiquitous and significant processes on Earth[1–6], can utilize light energy to drive chemical reactions, offering the potential for clean energy and playing a crucial role in environmental remediation and sustainable development[7–9]. The photocatalytic process usually involves the generation and conversion of molecules typically occurring at the nanoscale[10–13], which includes light absorption, generation of electron-hole pairs, separation and migration of electrons and holes, and surface reactions on its surface. Recently, more and more photocatalysts were fabricated based on nanoparticles[14–17], however, the understanding of the photocatalytic reactions occurring at the nanoscale is still an great challenges[18–20].

For various photocatalytic reactions, ranging from water splitting and CO2 reduction to organic synthesis reactions[18–21], photocatalytic water splitting is the most representative one, providing a model system for studying photocatalytic mechanisms[22–24]. Great efforts have been dedicated to understanding this process and developing highly efficient photocatalysts. On one hand, popular researches try to modify the band gap structure of the material or regulate the adsorption of the catalyst to the reactants. On the other hand, transient absorption spectroscopy, in-situ Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, in-situ Raman spectroscopy and other technologies are used to monitor the formation of intermediates during the reaction[25–27]. Also, there are many innovative studies analyzing the photocatalytic water splitting process from a kinetic perspective. For example, Liu et al. used carbon dots and carbon nitride nanocomposites (CDs/C3N4) for photocatalytic water decomposition, demonstrating a stepwise 2e-/2e- two-step catalytic process. For the first time, by establishing a new photo-induced current (TPC) method, they show that C3N4 dominates the first step of oxidation of H2O to H2O2, followed by a rapid second step (H2O2 disproportionation to O2) catalyzed by CDs[28]. Wu et al. designed and synthesized a carbon based photocatalyst (PM-CDs), achieving high-efficiency photo-synthesis of H2O2 from seawater[29]. In this work, the transient photovoltage (TPV) tests indicated the ionization of the functional groups enhances electron absorption properties and increases the electron extraction rate. The electron-hole recombination process is also prolonged because the trapped electrons surrounded by the functional groups form an energy barrier and cannot participate in the reaction in a short time. Liu et al. synthesized a metal-free catalyst for the photocatalytic production of H2O2, where the formative CDs on catalyst facilitate the generation, separation and transfer of photoinduced charges[30]. Wang et al. synthesized a metal-free carbon based photocatalyst with in-situ generated CDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis treatment[31]. In this work, a new transient potential scanning (TPS) method was established and the dynamics of interfacial charge distribution in photocatalytic processes were also discussed, bring a new understanding on the photocatalytic reactions.

Here, we first show the fabrication of two kinds of gold nanoclusters, namely, the gold nanoclusters stabilized traditional thiol ligands (AuNCs) and the gold nanoclusters stabilized with carbon dots (AuCDs). Then, we study their interfacial kinetics of photocatalytic H2O2 production in details. Specifically, we used three quasi in-situ testing methods to characterize the interfacial charge kinetic of the catalyst under working conditions. TPV tests were used to explore the charge transfer behavior of AuCDs and AuNCs under illumination conditions. Transient potential scanning (TPS) and transient photo-induced current (TPC) tests were performed to show the differences of interfacial charge distribution dynamics and the number of electron transfers during the catalytic process. The presence of CDs changes the way AuNCs consume photo generated holes by sacrificing their own ligands and provides the stable catalytic sites for water oxidation reaction (WOR), ensuring the stability of the catalyst. The operando analysis methods of TPV, TPS and TPC analyze the difference in the separation and migration of electrons and holes, reactant adsorption, reduction reaction rates and the issue of electron transfer number during the catalytic process, providing new methods for an in-depth understanding of catalytic kinetic processes.

Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4) and Glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Aladdin. Ethanol and HCl were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd and Chinasun Specialty Products Co., Ltd. Citric acid were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd. (China). Cellulose dialysis bag (500–1000 Da) was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co. Ltd. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water (resistivity:ρ ≥ 18 MΩ/cm) was used to prepare the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AuNCs, CDs and AuCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of AuNCs: Typically, 4 mL of HAuCl4 solution (500 mmol/L) was added into the flask. Then, the above solution was diluted to 346 mL with ultrapure water. Under a stirring speed of 600 rpm, 150 mL of GSH solution (20 mmol/L) was gently added to the previous solution. The obtained solution was then placed in a flask and heated at 85 ℃ for 3 hours. After the above operation was completed, the solution was filtered by using filters (0.22 µm) and then dialyzed in ultrapure water for 72 hours to obtain the product.

Synthesis of CDs: Briefly, 192 mg citric acid and 363 mg GSH were dissolved into 20 mL ultrapure water first. Then, the obtained solution was placed inside the hydrothermal reaction vessel and sonicated for 30 minutes. After a hydrothermal reaction at 160 ℃ for 4 h, the mixture solution with blue fluorescent emission was acquired. Afterward, the solution was dialyzed via cellulose dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da) for 7 days until no fluorescence was observed in the external dialysis solution. The dialyzed solution was used as the CDs solution for the subsequent synthesis of AuCDs.

Synthesis of AuCDs: The synthesis of AuCDs is similar to that of AuNCs. Briefly, 4 mL of HAuCl4 solution was diluted to 250 mL while maintaining a stirring speed of 600 rpm. Next, the CDs solution was dropwise added to the previous solution until the solution changed from yellow to brown. The obtained solution was heated at 85 ℃ for 3 hours in a flask. Subsequently, the yellow-light solution was filtered (0.22 µm) and dialyzed (500–1000 Da) to obtain the AuCDs.

Both AuNCs and AuCDs were synthesized by a one-pot wet chemistry method. The AuNCs catalysts use HAuCl4 as the Au source and GSH as the ligands, while for AuCDs, the GSH ligands were replaced with CDs. The morphology of samples was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 1 a shows the spherical morphology of AuNCs with size ranging from 2 to 4 nm. Additionally, AuNCs exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm[32], corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of the face-centered cubic (fcc) structure of gold[33,34]. The CDs show a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, attributed to the (100) crystal plane of graphite[35]. Fig. 1 b demonstrates that AuCDs possess a spherical morphology with sizes ranging from 4 to 7 nm, which is larger than the AuNCs. AuCDs have two types of lattice sizes, 0.21 nm and 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of the fcc structure of gold and the (100) crystal plane of graphite. Additional TEM images of CDs, AuNCs and AuCDs are shown in Fig. S1. Due to the uniformity of the samples (CDs, AuNCs, AuCDs) and their small size, there are no obvious characteristic peaks in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S2). The gold content and the ratio of gold to sulfur were quantified through inductively coupled plasma pectrometry (ICP) and are listed in Table S1.

Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) were used to study the samples’ absorption properties. In Fig. S3, the CDs have an obvious absorption peak located at 349 nm attributed to the n-π* transition, which is mainly assigned to double bonds including CS and CO in the CDs[35]. The AuNCs exhibit an absorption peak at about 385 nm which is attributed to the transition between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) (Fig. 1 c)[36]. Both the peaks attributed to CDs and AuNCs are displayed on the UV–vis spectrum of AuCDs (Fig. 1 d). The inset images of Figs. 1 c and 1 d provide the structural diagram of AuNCs and AuCDs, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to reflect the information of the functional groups in the samples. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S4) of GSH displays characteristic absorption bands of O-H (3422 cm−1), C-H (2921 cm−1), S-H (2531 cm−1), CO (1728 cm−1/1640 cm−1), C-N (1396 cm−1), CS (1050 cm−1) and C-S (744 cm−1), respectively. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. S4), the S-H stretching bands weakens. Moreover, the S-H stretching bands disappeared and the C-S stretching bands were enhanced in the FTIR spectra of AuNCs (Fig. 1 e) and AuCDs (Fig. 1 f)[37]. This may be due to the formation of Au-S bonds[38]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was used to investigate the optical fluorescence of the samples. The CDs (Figs. S5a and S5d) exhibit a fluorescence peak at the wavelength of 460 nm with the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The AuNCs (Figs. S5b and S5e) exhibit a maximum fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 630 nm with the excitation wavelength of 440 nm. The AuCDs (Figs. S5c and S5f) exhibit both the characteristic emission peak of CDs and AuNCs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were used to investigate the chemical and electronic states of the samples. The full survey spectrum of CDs (Fig. S6) shows binding energy peaks located at 284.6, 399.7, 532.2 and 163.2 eV, which are assignable to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and S 2p, respectively. This proves that CDs are composed of C, N, O and S elements. The high-resolution of Au 4 f XPS spectra of AuCDs and AuNCs are displayed in Figs. 1 g and 1 h. The spectra of AuNCs and AuCDs exhibit two peaks centered at 84.3 and 88.0 eV, belonging to Au 4 f5/2 and Au 4 f7/2, respectively. The binding energy peaks of Au 4 f5/2 and Au 4 f7/2 of bulk gold are located at 84.0 and 87.4 eV[39], respectively. Compared to bulk gold, there is an increase for the binding energies of Au 4 f5/2 and Au 4 f7/2 [40]. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum of AuNCs and AuCDs are illustrated in Fig. S7. The addition high-resolution C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s spectra of CDs, AuNCs and AuCDs are shown in Fig. S8. Compared to the standard sulfur element, the binding energy attributed to S 2p of AuNCs and AuCDs shift slightly towards a low energy direction. In addition, the peak at 167.9 eV is attributed to the oxidized sulfur species. Combining the FTIR data, the slightly change of the binding energy is attributed to S 2p and Au 4 f, which indicates the electrons of sulfur are captured by gold to form Au-S bond[41].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and Mott-Schottky tests were used to determine the band structure of samples. The CV curves of samples were obtained in N2 saturated acetonitrile solution with 100 mM tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAP) as electrolyte. The reference electrode was an Ag/Ag+ electrode (AgCl dissolved in acetonitrile). Ferrocene was used as the external standard, and the CV curve is shown in Fig. S9. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the conduction band potential (ECB) and valence band potential (EVB) of AuNCs are calculated to be −0.07 and 1.55 V vs. RHE. Form the CV curve shown in Fig. 2 b, the ECB and EVB of AuCDs are calculated as −0.04 V and 1.41 V vs. RHE[42]. The Mott-Schottky plots of materials were recorded at different frequencies. The positive and negative values observed in the Mott-Schottky plots signify that the samples exhibit both n-type and p-type semiconductor properties[43]. By analyzing the Mott-Schottky plots, the two flat-band potentials (Efb) of AuNCs (Fig. 2 c) are estimated to be −0.53 and 0.72 V vs. SCE, and the two Efb of AuCDs (Fig. 2 d) are estimated to be −0.73 and 0.66 V vs. SCE. Detailed Mott-Schottky plots are shown in Fig. S10. Furthermore, considering that the ECB of n-type semiconductors is typically negative by about 0.1 V to 0.3 V relative to the Efb value, and the EVB of p-type semiconductors is typically positive by about 0.1 V to 0.3 V relative to the Efb value (Detailed explanation can be found in Eqs. 1–3), the calculated ECB and EVB of AuNCs are −0.07 V vs. RHE and 1.51 V vs. RHE and the calculated ECB and EVB of AuCDs are −0.27 V vs. RHE and 1.58 V vs. RHE (ECB = Efb - 0.2 V, EVB = Efb + 0.2 V). These results indicate that the smaller Au clusters (only a few dozen atoms) constitutes discrete energy levels which lead to the specific locations of the LUMOs and HOMOs levels[44]. The changes of ligands did not significantly alter the energy level structure. Figs. 2 e and 2 f present the energy level diagram of AuNCs and AuCDs, revealing that the energy bands of AuNCs and AuCDs satisfy the potentials required for the O2→H2O2 reaction.

The transient photocurrent response (TPR) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves were used to illustrate the generation and transfer capability of photogenerated carriers[45]. According to Figs. 3 a and 3 b, the AuCDs have smaller internal resistance, which indicates that the AuCDs exhibit better electron-hole transfer ability than AuNCs. As shown in Figs. 3 c and 3 d, the AuCDs exhibit higher photocurrent intensity than AuNCs, which illustrates that the AuCDs have better electron-hole separation efficiency compared to AuNCs. Picosecond ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy (UTAS) was used to understand the optical characteristics of samples[46]. The AuCDs (Fig. 3 f) have two fluorescence peaks located at 470 nm and 680 nm corresponding to CDs and AuNCs (Fig. 3 e), respectively. As shown in Fig. S11, the photoexcitation of AuCDs has broader absorption than that of AuNCs. Meanwhile, the excited state absorption of AuNCs decays over time, while the excited state absorption of light by AuCDs remains relatively stable for a long period of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic properties of the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the photocatalytic ability of the catalysts for H2O2 production was investigated. The concentration of H2O2 was determined by a standard colorimetric method involving the eFOX solution, and the corresponding curve is shown in Fig. S12 [47]. There is no measurable presence of H2O2 when only CDs were used as a photocatalyst (Fig. S13). The time-dependent profiles of H2O2 production catalyzed by AuNCs are depicted in Fig. 4 a. The H2O2 production of AuNCs gradually decreases after 12 hours of catalytic reaction. For AuCDs (Fig. 4 b), there is no significant degradation in catalyst activity during 47 hours catalytic process, indicating that the AuCDs have good stability. Meanwhile, AuNCs are superior to AuCDs in terms of H2O2 production, which may be attributed to the faster rate of the ligands oxidation reaction of AuNCs compared to the hole depletion reaction of AuCDs. DMPO spin-trapping electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR) tests were used to detect products during H2O2 production processes. Figs. 4 c and 4 d illustrate that no detectable amount of superoxide radicals (·O2 -) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) are observed under dark conditions. However, under illuminated conditions, ·O2 - is detected, whereas ·OH is not. As revealed in Fig. 4 e, there is no measurable presence of H2O2 in the absence of light. In air-saturated environment, an amount of H2O2 is detected (547.76 µmol/L). In contrast, the concentration of H2O2 production measured in O2-saturated environment is 981.11 µmol/L, which is nearly twice as much as that in the air scenario. And in a N2-saturated environment, the concentration of H2O2 production is 33.02 µmol/L. For AuCDs (Fig. 4 f), there is also no detectable H2O2 under dark conditions. The concentration of H2O2 production is 194.22 µmol/L in N2-saturated environment. The concentration of H2O2 production measured in O2-saturated environment is 285.40 µmol/L, which is significantly higher than that under air atmosphere. These findings suggest that the photocatalytic H2O2 production reaction occurres via ORR process.

To verify the stability of the catalyst, AuNCs were subjected to six hours of photocatalytic H2O2 production, and the photocatalytic H2O2 production reaction was carried out for 12 hours on AuCDs. During the five cycles, the activity of AuNCs (Fig. 4 g) significantly decreased. In the second cycle, its activity dropped to half of the initial activity, and by the fifth cycle, its activity was reduced to 1/10 of the initial value. As shown in Fig. 4 h, AuCDs still maintained the catalytic activity after five cycles. From the aforementioned analysis, a comprehensive understanding can be deduced. For AuNCs as a photocatalyst, their surface ligands cannot resist the oxidation of photo-generated holes, leading to activity deactivation under light conditions. However, when CDs are used to replace ligands in the synthesis of clusters, the AuCDs are not consumed by photo-generated holes, thus achieving long-term stable clusters under illumination conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interfacial charge transfer kinetics of samples tested by TPV
ce:section.ce:para:
TPV testing was used to explore the interfacial charge transfer kinetics of catalysts. Charge extraction rate of photogenerated carriers (t
max ), electron decay constant (τ), maximum extraction amount (A) and effective charge extraction amount (n
e ) are regarded as indicators for evaluating the kinetics of interfacial charge transfer kinetics[30]. Figs. 5 a- 5 c are the original TPV data. After amplification and area integration processing of the original TPV data, it can be analyzed that the charge extraction rate of AuCDs (t
max2 = 0.0703 ms) is longer than AuNCs (t
max1 = 0.0371 ms) (Fig. 5 d, Table S2), indicating that AuCDs have slower charge separation rate. Additionally, the τ of AuCDs (τ
2 = 0.3053 ms) is also larger than AuNCs (τ
1 = 0.2593 ms) (Fig. 5 e, Table S2), which means the electron and hole recombination rate of AuCDs is longer than AuNCs. The maximum charge extraction efficiency (A) of AuCDs (A
2 = 0.0249) is lower than AuNCs (A
1 = 0.0306) (Fig. 5 f, Table S2), corresponding to less photogenerated charge on the surface of AuCDs. Moreover, the surface effective electron numbers (n
e = A*τ/t
max ) of the catalysts are calculated and listed in Table S2. It can be seen that the n
e of AuCDs (n
e2 = 0.1081) is noticeably low compared with AuNCs (n
e1 = 0.2139), indicating that the AuNCs show higher photocatalytic ability than AuCDs. However, for such an unstable catalyst of AuNCs, the behavior of ligand oxidation holes should also be considered. The higher photocatalytic ability due to the photo-generated hole consumption by ligand of AuNCs is greater than the rate of hole consumption reaction occurring on AuCDs. This also well explains that the catalytic activity of AuNCs in the initial stage is greater than that of AuCDs during activity testing.

To investigate the deactivation behavior of catalysts, AuNCs and AuCDs before and after the reaction were texted by TPV in the same testing environment. For AuNCs (Figs. 5 g- 5 i), after the reaction, the t
max3 , τ
3 and A
3 of AuNCs are significantly reduced. Compared to AuNCs before the reaction, the n
e of AuNCs after the reaction decreased by about 90 % (n
e1 = 0.2139, n
e3 = 0.0249). The oxidation of the ligand in AuNCs leads to the loss of protective effect of Au, resulting in the deactivation of AuNCs. However, for AuCDs (Figs. 5 j- 5 l), there is no significant change in the t
max , τ and A parameters. In addition, no decrease was found in the n
e before and after reaction (n
e2 = 0.1081, n
e4 = 0.1444). This indicates that AuCDs do not undergo oxidation of itself during the catalytic process, and the photo-generated holes participate in other catalytic reactions, which also ensures the long-term stability of the catalyst in the catalytic process. As shown in Figs. S14a and S14c, the characteristic oxidation peak attributed to GSH of AuNCs diminishes after catalytic reaction, which is due to the oxidation of the ligands. However, the characteristic oxidation peak of AuCDs has no changes, indicating that the presence of CDs ensures the stability of the catalyst and constitutes new energy levels (Figs. S14b and S14d). Meanwhile, the structure of AuNCs undergoes significant changes and aggregation is observed, but for AuCDs, there is no significant change in structure after catalytic reaction (Figs. S15 and S16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamics of electron distribution at catalytic interfaces texted by TPS
ce:section.ce:para: The TPS test was employed to analyze the interfacial charge distribution kinetics during the reaction. This test was typically conducted in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution saturated with N2/O2, under dark or illumination conditions. Based on the CV curves in Fig. S17, two test voltages of +0.8 V and −0.8 V were selected, as no redox reactions occur at these two voltages. In a semiconductor/solution system, applying negative (positive) potential results in electron injection, which alters the Fermi level in the interfacial layer[43,48]. When the applied negative (positive) potential reaches a certain threshold, the band bending disappears, minimizing the space charge layer thickness and creating a flat band that corresponds to the flat band potential[42,49]. In the present TPS system, the intersection point of the current curves under illumination and dark conditions indicates that the photogenerated current is approximately zero, corresponding to the flat band potential of the photocatalyst. At this point, the potential value of the V-t curve under illumination represents the true flat band potential of the catalyst under operational conditions, which differs from the flat band potential observed during material characterization under non-catalytic conditions.

The current curves with intersection points of AuNCs and AuCDs, and the corresponding voltage curves in O2-saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution under dark and illuminated conditions are displayed in Figs. 6 a- 6 d, respectively. The probability of a quantum state with energy E being occupied by an electron is:

For n-type semiconductors (donor doped), when at room temperature (around 300 K), the relationship between the Fermi level EF of the semiconductor and the CB bottom level Ec is:

In the formulae, EF is the Fermi level of the semiconductor, EC is the bottom level of the conduction band, ND is the concentration of donor impurities, and NC is the effective density of states in the conduction band. EV is the top level of the valence band, NA is the concentration of acceptor impurities, and NV is the effective density of states in the valence band. When the surface state density of semiconductors is high, it will cause the Fermi level pinning effect. The surface energy band of high surface state semiconductors will produce a fixed value bending. In the surface state, there exists an energy level about 1/3 of the bandgap width from the top of the surface valence band, above which all are acceptor levels and below which all are donor levels. Therefore, for n-type (p-type) catalyst materials, the conduction band of the photocatalyst is about 0.1–0.3 eV lower (higher) than flat band potential. Next the comparison of band structure of AuNCs and AuCDs under operational condition in different reaction systems is described in Figs. 6 e and 6 f. The ECB of AuNCs and AuCDs are 0.075 and 0.070 V vs. RHE and the EVB of AuNCs and AuCDs are 1.241 and 1.251 V vs. RHE under operational condition. This explains that although the conduction band of the catalyst satisfies the requirement of hydrogen evolution, it does not produce H2, because the potential position of photoelectrons on the catalyst surface does not reach 0 V vs. RHE under operational conditions. The valence band edges of AuNCs and AuCDs are slightly higher than other results from CV and Mott-Schottky analyses, but they still satisfied the requirement of the reaction (typical four-electron route: 2 H2O → O2 + 4e- + 4 H+).

Under the application of a potential of −0.8 V, the majority carriers in the system are electrons, and the main reaction that takes place is ORR. The charge consumption time (τ x ) under −0.8 V is investigated, which is related to the product of capacitance and resistance (τ x = RC)[31]. When the surface of the material adsorbs O2, the medium in the capacitor increases, leading to increased capacitance and prolonged decay time. However, at the same time, O2 located on the surface of the material also undergoes a life cycle reaction, forming fast charge transfer channels, resulting in a decrease in τ x values. The TPS curves for AuNCs and AuCDs are provided in Figs. 7 a- 7 d in different environments, respectively. The detailed value of τ x are reflected in Figs. 7 e and 7 f in different situations. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, in a dark situation, the τ x of AuNCs in O2-saturated situation is 0.021 s, which is higher than that in N2. For AuCDs (Fig. 7 b), the τ x in O2 is also greater than that in N2. This indicates that both AuNCs and AuCDs have an adsorption effect on O2, which can lead to an increase in the capacitance of the material surface. When illumination is applied to the AuNCs system (Fig. 7 c), the value of τ x declines in the O2-saturated system, revealing that the O2 adsorbed by AuNCs surface ligands is easily activated under light conditions. On the contrary, for the AuCDs system (Fig. 7 d), the τ x increases after illumination in an O2 atmosphere. This indicates that the O2 adsorbed on the surface of AuCDs is difficult to activate under light conditions, which may also be one of the reasons for the slow catalytic speed of AuCDs[46]. Thus, the data provides a conclusion: both AuNCs and AuCDs have a strong adsorption capacity for O2, but the O2 on the surface of AuNCs is easily activated by ligands, while the O2 on the surface of AuCDs tends to be stable and difficult to activate. This may also be the reason for the difference in catalytic activity between AuNCs and AuCDs.

The Bulter-Volmer (B-V) equation is used to calculate the rate at which the potential on the electrode changes with ion concentration. As shown in the following:
(4)
γ = nFkc

− ∆ G / RT
Where γ is the reaction rate, n is the number of electrons related to the reaction, F is Faraday constant, k is reaction rate constant, c is the concentration of reactant, ΔG is the overpotential, R is the gas constant, and T is the reaction temperature. In ORR, concentration of reactants includes the concentration of O2 and photoinduced electrons. The concentration of electrons corresponds to the difference value of current in TPS curve under light and dark condition. As mentioned earlier, in TPS system, the value of τ x is proportional to the concentration of absorbed O2. In addition, the overpotential is the difference between the conduction band of the catalyst under operational condition and the potential of ORR. Thus, the ratio of ORR rate of AuNCs and AuCDs can be calculated as Eq. 5 to Eq. 6:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transfer pathways of electrons studied by TPC
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the detailed reaction mechanism of H2O2 production of catalyst, rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) i-t curves in different testing environments were used to verify the transfer of electronic processes. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the ORR electron transfer number of AuNCs tested in acetonitrile containing 2 mg/L H2O is 0.5 (single-electron ORR process). However, for AuCDs, the calculated electron transfer number of the ORR is 1.91, which is close to 2 (Fig. 8 b, two-electron ORR process) in the same testing environment (the detailed calculation is shown in Supporting Information). To study the half reaction involving photo-generated holes, the WOR electron transfer number was tested in N2-saturated 0.1 M TBAP anhydrous acetonitrile solution. For blank electrodes, at the moment of illumination, no obvious current changes are detected on the disk electrode, but a constant photocurrent value (0.204 mA) is generated on the ring electrode due to the platinum ring's response to light. Due to AuNCs sacrificing holes by consuming their own ligands, in the absence of electrons and O2, even with illumination, no chemical reaction occurs on the disk electrode surface. This results in no significant current signal change being observed on the rotating ring-disk electrode at the moment of illumination. However, for AuCDs, the electron transfer number can be measured to be approximately 4, demonstrating that the photogenerated holes participate in the WOR (typical four-electron route: 2 H2O → O2 +4e- + 4 H+) under visible light radiation[50,51]. Therefore, the presence of CDs changes the consumption mode of photogenerated holes. Based on the above data, it can be concluded that the strategy of changing ordinary ligands to CDs has changed the reaction pathway of the ORR (from two-step and one electron to one-step and two electrons). At the same time, the hole half-reaction has changed from the original oxidation of its own ligands to a four-electron WOR, which also explains the catalytic stability of AuCDs compared to AuNCs.

In this work, a detailed and comprehensive comparison of the interfacial kinetics of photocatalytic H2O2 production over AuNCs and AuCDs catalysts was carried out by the combined operando study with three new methods: TPV, TPS and TPC. Specifically, in present system, the ligand consumption, the dynamics of interfacial charge distribution at catalytic interfaces, the adsorption and activation of O2, and the charge transfer path during catalytic prepossess were discussed in detail. Notably, with the addition of CDs, the ORR changes from a two-step one electron process to a one-step two electron process, and the hole consumption mode changes from consuming its own ligand to a four-electron water oxidation reaction. This work shows both a new approach for synthesizing stable clusters and a new comprehensive operando study of photocatalysis at the nanoscale.
